quote: Okay. Gotcha. In the Yeah. I mean, it okay.
stewart_read: Janet has just disclosed a pattern of broken promises — 'get instant code' that never arrives, followed by a bait-and-switch to a home visit. This is a trust-eroding process complaint, not a random objection. Carter's response is a verbal stumble that neither acknowledges the frustration nor differentiates Ion from the prior experience. The codex notes that customers who complain about contact frequency or process should be resolved via action, not defense. Carter does neither — he absorbs the complaint and pivots to the appointment ask. The protocols.excessive_contact_complaint gap (TBD in codex) is directly relevant here.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter is deliberately not dwelling on the prior frustration to avoid amplifying it, choosing instead to move forward and let the appointment quality speak for itself. Rejected — the verbal stumble ('In the Yeah. I mean, it okay.') signals genuine unpreparedness, not deliberate de-escalation.
coaching_implication: Show Carter the codex note on resolving process complaints via action. A one-sentence acknowledgment ('That's a fair frustration — the instant-code thing is a lead-gen tool, not us. What I can do is actually walk you through a real design.') would have converted the complaint into a differentiator. Manager should role-play this specific scenario: customer discloses prior broken promise, rep must acknowledge + reframe without dwelling.

quote: Well, are you gonna be able to install this thing before the texting runs out or not?
stewart_read: Janet's tax-credit deadline question is skeptical-transactional — she's probing whether this engagement is worth her time, not expressing enthusiasm. Carter answers affirmatively ('Yeah. We should be able to') and correctly routes the timeline detail to the appointment. The response is adequate but misses a flip opportunity: the urgency Janet is expressing could have been turned into a booking reason ('That's exactly why we should get you on the calendar now — the timeline is tight and the design takes a few days to build'). The emotional register here is 'prove it to me,' not 'I'm excited.'
alternative_hypothesis: Enthusiasm signal read: Janet is motivated by the tax credit and this signals high purchase intent. Rejected — the phrasing ('are you gonna be able to... or not?') is a challenge, not an expression of interest. The motivation is real but the tone is skeptical.
coaching_implication: Carter answered the question but left the urgency on the table. Manager should show Carter how to convert a deadline probe into a booking accelerant: acknowledge the timeline concern, confirm Ion can meet it, then immediately use that as the reason to lock the appointment now. The flip is available here and Carter didn't take it.

quote: What has you interested in solar?
stewart_read: Carter asks the codex's highest-depth verify item and receives 'We've been interested for a while' at 00:24. He responds with 'Cool.' and moves to the next question. The entire interest-in-solar exchange takes under five seconds. The codex is explicit: 'Top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Bottom setters check the box and move on.' Carter checks the box. There is no bill pain surfaced, no emotional anchor established, no curiosity created. The rest of the call proceeds without any emotional foundation — it's a data-collection exercise.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter is moving quickly because Janet has already signaled process fatigue and a long verify section would increase drop risk. Partially valid — but the codex's depth requirement on this item exists precisely because the emotional anchor it creates is what makes the appointment worth showing up to. Speed here trades a fragile booking for a solid one.
coaching_implication: This is the single highest-leverage coaching gap on the call. Manager should pull the codex's verify.interest_in_solar note and show Carter the contrast between 'Cool' and a 60-second depth probe. A suggested follow-up after 'We've been interested for a while': 'What's been the main driver — the bill, or something else?' Even one follow-up question would have surfaced bill pain or a specific motivator Carter could have anchored the entire pitch to.

stewart_read: Janet states her average monthly bill as $4.34. Carter responds 'K.' and moves to the next verify item. This is a double failure: first, $4.34 is almost certainly a data error (possibly a daily rate, a partial bill period, or a misread) and Carter does not probe it; second, even if the number were accurate, the codex's 'ask + acknowledge + flip' pattern requires an emotional flip against the utility — Carter does 'ask + acknowledge + move on.' The bill anchor is the foundation of the entire pitch. Carter has neither validated the number nor used it.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter recognizes the number is anomalous but decides to let the closer handle it rather than derail the call with a clarifying detour. Rejected — there is no evidence of strategic awareness here; the response is a flat 'K.' with no flag, no probe, and no note for the closer.
coaching_implication: Two separate coaching beats here. First: when a bill number is implausible, probe it ('$4.34 — is that a daily charge or the full monthly bill? Just want to make sure we're working with the right number'). Second: even a plausible bill number needs the flip. Manager should walk Carter through the 'ask + acknowledge + flip' pattern with a concrete example using Janet's number, whatever it actually is.

quote: I'll be here. He won't be until it's, like, an issue.
stewart_read: Michael Dewar is on the title. Janet signals he won't be available. Carter probes once ('When would he be around?') and when Janet explains his schedule is unpredictable, Carter immediately books without him ('Okay. Yeah. No worries then.'). The codex is unambiguous: co-title-holders must be on the appointment. The correct move is not to overcome the objection but to find a time that works for both. Carter treats this as a scheduling inconvenience rather than a structural requirement.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter judges that pushing harder on Michael's inclusion will lose the booking entirely, and a one-title-holder appointment is better than no appointment. Possible — but the codex explicitly warns that 'setters who win this objection lose the deal.' The strategic play is to find a time that works for Michael, not to book without him.
coaching_implication: Manager should walk Carter through the protocols.spouse_decision template verbatim: 'This is the kind of decision both of you should be part of — what time works for both of you?' The SESSION18_fd078269 positive example (Randy/Juan booked in 4 min by reframing the appointment as the spouse-inclusion venue) is the reference case. Carter needs to understand that booking without Michael is not a win — it's a fragile sit that is likely to no-close.

stewart_read: Carter stays on the line through the ~76-second bill-send window (03:52 to 04:42) rather than scheduling a callback or giving out a personal number. This is correct execution — the bill is captured live, the appointment is booked in the same call, and no personal phone number is exchanged. This is the one clean procedural win on the call and worth naming explicitly so the manager can reinforce it.
coaching_implication: Reinforce this explicitly in the 1-on-1. Carter held the line through the bill-send friction and got the bill live. That's the right move and it's not universal on the floor. Name it so Carter knows it's a strength to build on, not just a lucky outcome.

stewart_read: Carter's button-up is missing two required elements: (1) the no-driving/at-computer anchor ('The only thing we ask is that you aren't driving and that you'll be able to sit down to review the design on your computer') and (2) the 'Is that fair?' verbal handshake. The codex notes that skipping the computer anchor raises no-show rate. This is especially consequential here because Janet's $4.34 bill anomaly means the closer will need Janet to pull up her actual utility account during the appointment — without the computer anchor, that review is at risk.
coaching_implication: Manager should drill the full button_up checklist with Carter. The no-driving/computer anchor is not optional — it's a no-show prevention tool. Given Janet's bill anomaly, the closer will almost certainly need her at a computer. Carter should have set that expectation explicitly.

quote: Alright. Thank you. Awesome. Yep. Yep. Of course. Have a good one. You too, sir. Bye.
stewart_read: Carter closes the call without confirming a follow-up text. The codex lists the text-followup promise as a required button-up element, and the text template requires a tieback to something specific the customer said. Given Janet's process skepticism ('every time I fill something out... I get no instant code'), a personalized confirmation text is not just a nice-to-have — it's a trust signal that differentiates Ion from the prior experiences she described. Carter's failure to promise it leaves the appointment without a written anchor.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter may send the text without promising it on the call. Rejected — the codex requires the promise to be made on the call, not just the text to be sent. The promise itself is part of the trust-building mechanism.
coaching_implication: Manager should add the text-followup promise to Carter's button-up checklist review. The tieback requirement is especially important here: the text should reference Janet's tax-credit timeline concern ('I have you on for tomorrow at 12PM to go over your design and the timeline for getting installed before the end of the year') — that's the specific hook that will make her show up.
